article_meta.title: The Corner NR PLUS Trade Breaking News: Americans Pay the Tariffs Their Government Imposes on Them By John R

reasoning: The model classifies this as Lean Right with 0.85 confidence. The article is an opinion piece from National Review arguing against tariffs from a free-market conservative perspective. This is a reasonable classification — the piece reflects traditional conservative/libertarian economics (invoking Adam Smith, opposing government intervention in trade). The key_indicators all cite the author's own prose, no attribution violations. The classification could be debated — one might argue Right given National Review's positioning and the strongly ideological free-market framing — but Lean Right is defensible for an opinion piece that criticizes a Republican president's signature policy. The confidence of 0.85 is reasonable for an opinion piece with clear ideological direction. Minor issue: the reasoning mentions the outlet (National Review) which shouldn't be a primary factor, but the author's prose choices are also cited.

custom_reasoning.classification_accuracy: The article is from National Review and argues a free-market conservative position against tariffs. Lean Right is defensible — the piece criticizes Trump from the right on economic policy, invoking Adam Smith and classical liberalism. One could argue Right given the unambiguous conservative ideological framework, but for an opinion piece the instructions say to score somewhat lower in magnitude. Lean Right is within one adjacent class of Right, so at worst this is one class off.
custom_reasoning.confidence_calibration: 0.85 confidence for a clear opinion piece with obvious ideological direction is appropriate. The piece is unambiguously free-market conservative, making 0.85 well-calibrated.
custom_reasoning.reasoning_evidence_quality: All key_indicators cite the author's own prose: 'Turns out Adam Smith got it right,' 'purported free lunch of Trump's tariffs is a farce,' 'Can you identify the logical reasoning there?' — all are the author's editorial language. No attribution violations. The reasoning also correctly identifies the piece as opinion/editorial. Minor weakness: mentioning National Review as a factor, but this is supplementary context rather than the primary basis.
